preamble: The transformation of the alveolar trill [r] into the voiceless velar fricative [x] is a documented phonetic shift, primarily observed as a result of rhotic lenition and backing. This change involves a shift in the place of articulation from the alveolar ridge to the velum, a change in manner from a trill to a fricative, and often a devoicing process if the original trill was voiced.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (trill to fricative), backing (alveolar to velar), and devoicing

examples.note: The phoneme /r/ is realized as [x] in initial, medial, and final positions, particularly when Singkil speakers interact with other Singkil speakers. For instance, 'rupanya' (meaning 'apparently') would be pronounced with an initial [x] instead of [r].

In the Massif Central region of France, the shift from the alveolar trill [r] to the voiceless velar fricative [x] is an ongoing process, with younger generations predominantly using [x] while older generations retain [r].

In various dialects of Brazilian Portuguese, the rhotic phoneme, particularly the 'strong r' (often historically from geminate 'rr' or word-initial 'r'), has shifted to a voiceless velar fricative [x] or even a glottal fricative [h].

sources: Suhery, D. & S. (2014). Phonological Contrasts in Bahasa Indonesia and Singkil Language. LINGPOET (Journal of Linguistics and Literary Research), 1(1), 1-10.
Suhery, D. & S. (2015). Social Contexts of Phonological Contrasts and Indexicality: Variability and Identity Among Singkils. Neliti.
Schiller, N. O. (2004). The phonetic variation of German /r/. In J. Gussenhoven & N. Warner (Eds.), The Phonetics and Phonology of the German Language (pp. 263-280). Oxford University Press.
Blust, R. (2020). Examples of reductive primary splits. Freelance reconstruction.
